title: Balance of payments & exchange rates
summary:
## The big idea No economy is an island. Egyptians buy Japanese cars, tourists buy nights on the Red Sea, Gulf investors buy Cairo apartments — and every one of those transactions is logged in the **balance of payments (BoP)** and settled in the **foreign exchange market**. This chapter connects your macro model to the rest of the world: net exports feed AD, and the exchange rate feeds both competitiveness and inflation. ## The balance of payments The **BoP** records **all economic transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world** over a period. Money flowing *in* is a credit; money flowing *out* is a debit. ### The current account (the AS focus) Four sections — learn them in order: 1. **Trade in goods** (*visible* trade): exports minus imports of physical goods — cars, oil, wheat. 2. **Trade in services** (*invisible* trade): tourism, transport, finance, insurance. 3. **Primary income:** investment income and wages crossing borders — dividends from shares abroad, profits repatriated by multinationals, interest. 4. **Secondary income (current transfers):** money moved with nothing given in return — workers' **remittances**, foreign aid. Add the four together: outflows bigger than inflows → a **current account deficit**; the reverse → a **surplus**. ### The other half: capital and financial accounts Investment flows — **FDI** (building factories abroad), portfolio flows (buying foreign shares/bonds), banking flows and changes in official **reserves** — sit in the financial (and small capital) account. By construction the whole BoP **balances**: a country running a current account deficit must attract matching **financial inflows** (borrowing, selling assets) to pay for it. That is *why* a persistent deficit can worry policymakers — it means building up debts to, or selling assets to, foreigners. ## Exchange rates The **exchange rate** is the **price of one currency in terms of another** — and in a **floating** system it is set exactly like any other price: by demand and supply, here in the foreign exchange (forex) market. Who **demands** the currency (pushing it up)? Foreigners buying the country's **exports**, tourists visiting, investors buying its assets. Who **supplies** it (pushing it down)? Residents buying **imports**, holidaying abroad, investing overseas — they sell home currency to get foreign currency.
**What each part means:** the 'good' being traded is the currency itself; its price (vertical axis) is the exchange rate. **D** comes from foreigners needing the currency (to buy exports and assets); **S** comes from residents selling it (to buy imports and foreign assets). A surge in export demand or investor confidence shifts demand from **D1** to the dashed **D2**: the currency **appreciates** from **ER1** to **ER2**. A fall in demand — or a rise in supply as residents import more — would do the opposite: **depreciation**.
**Vocabulary that examiners police:** in a *floating* system market forces cause **appreciation/depreciation**; in a *fixed* system a government decision causes **revaluation/devaluation**. ## What a change in the exchange rate does Two mnemonics carry the logic: - **Depreciation → WPIDEC** (**W**eak **P**ound: **I**mports **D**ear, **E**xports **C**heap). Exports gain competitiveness, imports cost more → net exports (X−M) tend to improve → AD rises. The sting: dearer imports feed **cost-push, 'imported' inflation**. - **Appreciation → SPICED** (**S**trong **P**ound: **I**mports **C**heap, **E**xports **D**ear). Cheaper imports ease inflation; exporters lose competitiveness. **The elasticity catch:** a depreciation only improves the trade balance if demand for exports and imports is responsive enough (formally the **Marshall–Lerner condition**, developed at A2). In the short run demand is inelastic — contracts are signed, habits stick — so the deficit often *worsens first, then improves*: the **J-curve**. ## Real-world application Sterling's plunge after the 2016 Brexit vote helped exporters but raised UK import prices. The Turkish lira and Egyptian pound depreciations of recent years show the inflation channel vividly — import-dependent economies feel a falling currency in the supermarket within weeks. China, meanwhile, long *managed* the yuan to keep exports competitive — a running source of trade friction with the USA.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **BoP** = all transactions with the rest of the world; **current account** = goods + services + primary income + secondary income (remittances). - Deficit on current account ⇒ matching **financial-account inflows** (the BoP balances overall). - **Exchange rate** = price of a currency, set by D (exports, inward investment) and S (imports, outward investment). - Floating: **appreciation/depreciation**; fixed: **revaluation/devaluation**. - **WPIDEC**: depreciation → exports cheap, imports dear → AD↑ but imported inflation. **SPICED** = the reverse. - Effect on trade balance depends on **elasticities** (Marshall–Lerner); short run often worsens first (**J-curve**).

keyTerms.definition: A record of all economic transactions between a country and the rest of the world.
The part of the BoP recording trade in goods and services, plus primary and secondary income.
When the outflows on the current account exceed the inflows.
The price of one currency expressed in terms of another.
A fall in a currency's value in a floating exchange-rate system.
A rise in a currency's value in a floating exchange-rate system.
A deliberate fall/rise in a currency's value under a fixed exchange-rate system.

diagrams: **FOREX market:** axes = exchange rate (price of the currency) and quantity of the currency; downward demand, upward supply; show demand/supply shifts causing appreciation or depreciation.
Optional: label the **current account structure** (goods, services, primary and secondary income) as a table.
tips: Know the **four parts of the current account**; goods = visible, services = invisible trade.
Use **WPIDEC / SPICED** to recall the effects of depreciation/appreciation quickly and correctly.
State that the trade-balance effect **depends on elasticities** (Marshall–Lerner / J-curve) — a strong evaluation link.
Apply a real case (**UK sterling post-Brexit, the Turkish lira, or China's managed yuan**) for high-quality application marks (imported inflation vs competitiveness).

keyConcepts.concept: The current account of the BoP
keyConcepts.title: Balance of payments explained

revisionNotes:
# Balance of payments & exchange rates — quick revision

**Current account (4 parts, in order)**
1. **Goods** (visible) 2. **Services** (invisible) 3. **Primary income** (investment income, wages) 4. **Secondary income** (remittances, aid).
- Outflows > inflows → **deficit**; financed by **financial account** inflows (FDI, portfolio, reserves) — the BoP always balances overall.

**Exchange rate basics**
- Price of one currency in another; floating rate set by **D** (exports, tourism, inward investment) and **S** (imports, outward investment) of the currency.
- Floating → appreciation/depreciation. Fixed → revaluation/devaluation (policy decision).

**Effects**
- **Depreciation (WPIDEC)**: exports cheap, imports dear → (X−M)↑ → AD↑; but **imported cost-push inflation**.
- **Appreciation (SPICED)**: imports cheap (inflation eases), exports dear (competitiveness falls).
- **Marshall–Lerner**: depreciation improves the trade balance only if export+import demand is elastic enough; short-run inelasticity → **J-curve** (worse first, better later).

**Applications** — post-2016 sterling; Turkish lira & Egyptian pound (imported inflation); China's managed yuan.

**Exam habits**
- Put the right transaction in the right account section (remittances = secondary income!).
- Label the forex diagram: axes 'price of currency' & 'quantity of currency'.
- Evaluate depreciation with elasticities + imported inflation.
questions:
# Practice questions — Balance of payments & exchange rates **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Remittances sent home by citizens working abroad are recorded in the current account under: - A — trade in services - B — primary income - C — secondary income - D — the financial account
▸ Show answer

C. Remittances are transfers with nothing exchanged in return → secondary income. Primary income covers investment income and cross-border wages earned by short-term workers; the financial account covers investment flows.

**2.** A depreciation of a country's floating exchange rate will tend to make: - A — exports cheaper abroad and imports dearer at home - B — exports dearer abroad and imports cheaper at home - C — both exports and imports cheaper - D — both exports and imports dearer
▸ Show answer

A. WPIDEC: a weaker currency means foreigners pay less of their money for our goods, while foreign goods cost more of ours.

**3.** Which of the following would cause a country's currency to appreciate? - A — residents importing more foreign cars - B — a rise in foreign demand for the country's exports - C — residents investing more abroad - D — a fall in tourist arrivals
▸ Show answer

B. Export buyers must purchase the currency first — demand for it shifts right → appreciation. A, C and D all increase supply of (or cut demand for) the currency.

**4.** A country runs a persistent current account deficit. This must be matched by: - A — a surplus on its financial account - B — a deficit on its financial account - C — rising exports - D — an appreciation of its currency
▸ Show answer

A. The BoP balances overall: spending more abroad than you earn requires net inflows of finance — borrowing from foreigners or selling them assets.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A small economy relies on tourism and imported fuel and food. After political unrest cuts tourist arrivals by a third, its currency depreciates 20%. Import prices jump, pushing inflation from 4% to 12%. The trade deficit initially widens; eighteen months later, exports of textiles are up 15% and tourism has begun to recover, attracted by the cheaper currency. **(a)** Define 'depreciation' of a currency. **[2]** **(b)** Explain, with the aid of a demand and supply diagram of the foreign exchange market, why the currency depreciated. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse why the trade deficit widened at first and improved later. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the depreciation has been beneficial for this economy. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) A fall in the value of a currency against others in a floating system, caused by market forces. [1+1]

(b) Tourists buy the currency to spend locally → fewer tourists = demand for the currency shifts left → lower equilibrium exchange rate. Correct axes and shift. [4]

(c) J-curve logic: short run, demand for imports (fuel, food — necessities) and exports is inelastic → import bill rises with little volume change → deficit widens. Over time elasticities rise: textile exports +15%, tourism recovering → balance improves (Marshall–Lerner satisfied long run). [6]

(d) Benefits: competitiveness, export-led recovery, tourism revival. Costs: 12% inflation hits real incomes (import-dependent poor hardest), uncertainty, possible wage–price spiral. Judgement: painful short run, helpful adjustment long run — depends on inflation being contained. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the components of the current account of the balance of payments. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether a current account deficit is always a problem for an economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Goods, services, primary income, secondary income — with an example of each and the deficit/surplus definition.

(b) Not a problem when: financed by stable FDI inflows, caused by importing capital goods that raise future output, small relative to GDP. A problem when: persistent, financed by short-term 'hot money', reflects lost competitiveness, builds external debt. Judgement: size, cause and financing decide.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain, with the aid of a diagram, how a floating exchange rate is determined. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether a depreciation of its currency will always improve a country's trade balance. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) D for currency (exports, tourism, inward investment) and S (imports, outward investment); equilibrium rate; shift example.

(b) For: WPIDEC boosts export volumes, curbs imports. Against: Marshall–Lerner may fail short run (J-curve); inelastic import needs (fuel, food); imported inflation erodes the competitiveness gain; retaliation/global demand matters. Judgement: usually helps eventually, but conditionally.
